topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white of the lab results swam before Sarah’s eyes. She held the printout, the ink blurring slightly under the harsh fluorescent lights of the waiting room. Every line of the comparison, every percentage, felt like a tiny, sharp pinprick against her skin. Her hands, usually steady when she was working on her intricate coding projects, were trembling so violently she had to press them flat against the cool laminate table. A low, tight knot formed in her stomach, constricting her breath. *This can't be right. Impossible.* She reread the section detailing the genetic markers, her focus fracturing as a wave of dizzying uncertainty washed over her. When the nurse finally called her name, Sarah rose too quickly, the movement jerky, and felt a sudden, intense need to flee the room, her gaze darting around as if expecting someone to materialize and demand an explanation she wasn't prepared to give.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the screen, the confirmation blinking with an unnerving finality. The data confirmed it: the match was undeniable. A cold, clammy sensation spread from his fingertips up his arms, leaving him feeling strangely disconnected from his own body. He gripped the edge of the desk so hard his knuckles bleached white. He swallowed, the movement catching in his throat like dry sand. "This isn't how this was supposed to go," he managed, his voice sounding thin and reedy, a stark contrast to the booming confidence he usually projected. He leaned back abruptly, the chair groaning in protest, and began running a hand repeatedly through his hair, a nervous, repetitive motion. He kept glancing toward the closed door, listening intently for any sound, his entire posture radiating a tense, coiled stillness.

[story 3]

Elara sat in the quiet of her apartment, the DNA report spread across her lap like a map of uncharted territory. She kept touching the paper, tracing the lines with a fingertip, seeking some anchor in the overwhelming tide of confusion. A shallow, rapid breathing pattern filled her chest, making it difficult to draw a deep, satisfying breath. Every nerve ending seemed hyper-alert, vibrating with an unspoken anxiety. She stood up, pacing the small confines of the living room, her footsteps echoing too loudly on the hardwood floor. She stopped by the window, pressing her forehead against the cool glass, her jaw tight. The world outside seemed too loud, too bright, and she felt profoundly unsettled, as if the very air itself was charged with unspoken questions.
